name: Abdullah ibn Rawahah
title: Abdullah ibn Rawahah (RA): The Warrior Poet
meta_description: The story of Abdullah ibn Rawahah (RA), the first poet of the Prophet Muhammad, a warrior on the front line and a man who once invited a friend to "believe for an hour." His worship, his verses, and his death at Mutah.
keywords: Abdullah ibn Rawahah, story of Abdullah ibn Rawahah, poet of the Prophet, Battle of Mutah, the warrior poet, Ansar, sahaba stories
lede: He was a poet in a world where poets stayed far from the fighting. He was a warrior who chanted his verses out loud in the front line, while the swords were already swinging.

This is the story of Abdullah ibn Rawahah (may Allah be pleased with him): the first poet of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, a man whose worship was as fierce as his courage, and who walked toward his own death telling himself to stop clinging to this world.

sections.h2: A quick mind, and a single word
paras: Abdullah was from the Ansar, from the tribe of Khazraj in the city that would come to be called Madinah. His father had died before Islam reached them. He was one of the few among his people who could read and write, and he was known for verses so fine that the Prophet ﷺ had heard and loved them before the two had ever met.

People marvelled at how quickly his mind worked. He could glance at a handful of coins and name their worth without weighing them. He could look at a stranger and read where he was from, who his people were, what kind of man he might be. He was not easily impressed, and he was not a man who could be fooled. So when news of the Prophet ﷺ reached the city, there was real curiosity. What would a man this sharp make of the one they were calling a prophet?

When Abdullah finally saw him, he did not hesitate. He composed his first verses on the spot, and their meaning was simple: even without the signs, the revelation and the miracles, you would only have to watch the way this man carried himself, the way he answered, his whole bearing, to know he was a recipient of revelation from God.

It is a striking thing to notice. A rabbi who had spent years reading the old descriptions saw the Prophet ﷺ and said, that is him. Here was Abdullah, a man who judged people for a living, arriving at the same word from pure intelligence: that is him. He accepted Islam at once, travelled to Makkah, and was among the small group of Ansar, seventy-three of them, who pledged themselves to the Prophet ﷺ before the migration had even taken place.

Back in Madinah, Abdullah began calling people to Islam before the Prophet ﷺ had even arrived. He had a straightforward, no-nonsense way about him. He was the first to stand up to the man who had expected to be crowned king of the city, and whose plans Islam had quietly undone.

At one gathering, that man insulted the Prophet ﷺ and told him to keep his distance. Abdullah was the one who stood and said the opposite: we are very interested in what you have to say, so come and address us. He would not let an insult stand unanswered.

Because he was so trustworthy, the Prophet ﷺ used him as a messenger to the tribes inside Madinah. At Khaybar, it was Abdullah who was sent to estimate the date palms and divide the harvest fairly between the Muslims and the people of the land, and he was scrupulously fair in it. It was Abdullah, too, who was sent ahead to announce the victory of Badr to the people of Madinah, and he did it in his own poetic way.

sections.h2: The poet the Prophet ﷺ would quote
paras: Of the three poets who defended the Prophet ﷺ, Abdullah is the one with the most virtues recorded on the Prophet's own tongue. The scholars note something rare: the Prophet ﷺ would actually quote his lines. One he loved said that good news often reaches you from the very places and people you least expected it from.

There was a difference in how Abdullah wrote. The others would answer the enemy in their own currency, raising up their tribes and putting down others. Abdullah did not bother much with tribes. He raised up faith over disbelief, the honour of Islam over the emptiness of idol worship. That was his whole subject. His war poetry was not gentle, and he never pretended it was. It was meant to lift the morale of frightened, exhausted men in the worst hours of a battle, and he chanted it from the front line, not the back.

When the Muslims dug the great trench around Madinah, starving and afraid that one breach would finish them, the Prophet ﷺ began to raise his voice with Abdullah's lines. The words were a prayer as much as a poem: were it not for You we would never have been guided, nor given charity, nor prayed; so send down calm upon us, and if the enemy comes against us, we will not flee. One man took it up, then more, until the whole company was chanting it as they dug.

Years later, when the Muslims entered Makkah for the delayed pilgrimage, the Quraysh thought they were humbling them. Abdullah walked in answering them with poetry, making clear that even without weapons in hand the Muslims did not fear them. There was a place for that boldness, against the very people who had driven them out, and the Prophet ﷺ let him have it.

sections.h2: "Come, let us believe for an hour"
paras: What sets Abdullah apart is that, for all his sharp tongue and his place on the battlefield, his worship never slipped. He is the poet about whom no fault before the Prophet ﷺ is recorded. The Prophet ﷺ put him in charge of Madinah at times, kept him near in battle after battle, and placed him over many expeditions, where he led men while chanting his verses.

He used to take a companion by the arm and say, come, let us believe for an hour. He meant: let us sit together and remember Allah, let us refresh our faith for a while. Once a man misunderstood him, thinking he was belittling faith, and complained to the Prophet ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ smiled and said that Allah loves the gatherings the angels boast about, and asked that this be one of them.

His care to obey ran very deep. He once heard the Prophet ﷺ, from the pulpit, tell the people to sit down, and he sat down at once where he stood, out in the heat, rather than take another step. The Prophet ﷺ corrected the action gently, but praised the heart behind it: that instinct to obey the moment the command was heard.

sections.h2: A man who feared the verses he read
paras: His fear of Allah was tender and real. His wife once found him weeping, and began to weep herself. When he asked her why, she said only that she cried because he was crying. He told her he had just understood a verse: that every one of us would approach the Fire. The thought that it might be him was enough to break him.

He wept again at the verse about poets who are followed by those gone astray, and said that Allah knew he was a poet who might lead people astray. But the verse did not end there, and Allah comforted him with its conclusion.

It is a quiet lesson in how to read the Quran. Where another might say, that warning is not about me, Abdullah heard it and asked, what if it is about me? He turned the words on himself, and found mercy waiting in them.

ayah.text: Not so those [poets] who believe, do good deeds, remember God often, and defend themselves after they have been wronged. The evildoers will find out what they will return to.

sections.h2: Mutah, and two prayers at the door
paras: Abdullah died at the Battle of Mutah, one of the hardest the Muslims faced in the Prophet's lifetime. A force of three thousand met an army many times its size. The Prophet ﷺ had named the leaders in order: Zayd, then Ja'far, then Abdullah ibn Rawahah. To be third in that line was itself a station.

Zayd was killed, and Ja'far was killed, and Abdullah was urging the men forward in verse. When he was told the banner was now his, he felt himself hesitate, and answered his own soul out loud. Do I see you resisting Paradise? he asked it. By Allah, you will enter it, willingly or by force. Then he took the banner, advanced, and was killed. When the news reached Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ was weeping. He climbed the pulpit in tears to name the three who had been taken, one beloved name after another, all at once.

After his death, his widow remarried, and her new husband, hearing that her first husband had been a legend, wanted to know everything about him. What was he like at home? What was his worship like? Her answer was simple. The one thing she remembered was that he never entered the house without praying two units of prayer, and never left it without praying two. He never broke that habit.

He left no children. But he left a friend he had brought into Islam with his own insistence, and that friend never forgot it. For years after, the man would say that he sought refuge in Allah from ever doing a deed that would shame him before Abdullah, because the dead receive news of the living, and he could not bear for his brother to hear something wrong of him. That is the whole shape of the man: a poet whose words steadied an army, a warrior who feared a verse, a friend whose memory kept another man honest long after he was gone.

lessons_heading: What Abdullah ibn Rawahah's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His gifts could have made him proud. Instead they made him useful, and they never pulled him away from his Lord.

lessons.text: He did not hoard his talent or perform it for applause. He turned his poetry into courage for tired men and into praise for what is true.
When he read a hard verse, he did not assume it was about someone else. He asked what if it is about me, and that honesty kept his heart soft.
"Come, let us believe for an hour." A walk, a quiet gathering, a moment of remembrance shared with a friend can do more than a lecture.
Two units of prayer entering the house, two on leaving it. His faith lived in steady, unglamorous habits, not in grand gestures.

faq.a: A companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Ansar of Madinah, known as the first poet of the Prophet ﷺ. He was a warrior who chanted his verses in battle, a trusted messenger, and one of the leaders at the Battle of Mutah, where he was martyred.

faq.q: Why is he called the warrior poet?
faq.a: Poets of his time usually stayed away from fighting. Abdullah was both: he composed and recited poetry and also fought on the front line, chanting verses to raise the courage of the Muslims during battle.

faq.q: What does "let us believe for an hour" mean?
faq.a: It was his invitation to a friend to sit together and remember Allah, to refresh their faith for a while. When someone misunderstood it, the Prophet ﷺ explained that such gatherings of remembrance are beloved to Allah.

faq.q: How did Abdullah ibn Rawahah die?
faq.a: He was martyred at the Battle of Mutah. After the first two commanders, Zayd and Ja'far, were killed, Abdullah took the banner as the third leader, urged the men forward, and was killed in the fighting.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Abdullah ibn Rawahah had every reason to think well of himself: a quick mind, a famous tongue, a place at the Prophet's side. Yet the thing that defined him was not his brilliance. It was that he kept turning every gift back toward Allah, and kept fearing he had not done enough.

Most of us will not fight in a battle or compose a single line of verse. But all of us have something we are good at, and all of us read or hear words that could land on our own hearts if we let them. His life is an invitation to take what you have been given and put it to good use, to take a warning personally instead of passing it on, and to keep the quiet habits that hold a soul steady. Carry one of those into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abdullah ibn Rawahah (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Qur'an 26:227). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was a poet.

In his world, poets stayed far from the fighting.

He chanted his verses in the front line, while the swords were already swinging.

He had a quick mind.

He could glance at a handful of coins and name their worth. He could read a stranger's whole story from his face.

He was not a man who could be fooled.

When he finally saw the Prophet ﷺ, he did not hesitate.

From pure intelligence, reading the man's bearing, he arrived at one word:

**That is him.**

His war poetry steadied frightened men.

In the trench, starving and afraid, the Prophet ﷺ raised his voice with Abdullah's lines:

"Were it not for You we would never have been guided."

His favourite invitation to a friend:

**"Come, let us believe for an hour."**

A walk. A quiet gathering. A moment of remembrance shared.

He read a verse warning of those led astray, and wept, fearing it was about him.

Where another might say "that is not me," he asked, "what if it is me?"

At Mutah, the banner came to him and he hesitated.

Then he answered his own soul: "Do I see you resisting Paradise? By Allah, you will enter it."

He advanced, and was martyred.

**Let small habits hold you.**

Two units of prayer entering his house. Two on leaving it. He never broke it.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Abdullah ibn Rawahah (may Allah be pleased with him) was the first poet of the Prophet ﷺ, and the only one of the three poets about whom no fault was ever recorded. He was a warrior who chanted his verses from the front line, and a man whose worship was as fierce as his courage.

He used to take a friend by the arm and say, "Come, let us believe for an hour." He wept when he read a verse he feared was about him. And when he walked toward his death at Mutah, he told his own soul to stop clinging to this world.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The poet who chanted in the front line
email.alt_subject: "Come, let us believe for an hour"
email.preview: He was a warrior and a poet, and his worship never once slipped.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

In a time when poets kept far from the fighting, one companion was both poet and warrior. Abdullah ibn Rawahah (may Allah be pleased with him) chanted his verses from the front line of the battle, lifting the courage of frightened, exhausted men.

He had a famously quick mind. He could glance at a handful of coins and name their worth, and read a stranger's whole story from his face. He was not a man who could be fooled. So when he finally saw the Prophet ﷺ, and arrived from pure intelligence at the words "that is him," it carried weight.

When the Muslims dug the trench around Madinah, starving and afraid, the Prophet ﷺ raised his voice with Abdullah's lines: were it not for You we would never have been guided, nor prayed, nor given charity.

What moves me most about him is not his courage. It is his worship. He used to take a friend by the arm and say, "Come, let us believe for an hour." He meant: let us sit and remember Allah for a while. And when he read a verse warning of poets led astray, he wept, certain it might be about him. Where another might say "that is not me," he asked, "what if it is me?"

He died at the Battle of Mutah. The first two leaders were killed, and when the banner came to him, he felt himself hesitate. So he answered his own soul out loud: "Do I see you resisting Paradise? By Allah, you will enter it." Then he advanced, and was martyred.

After his death, his widow was asked what he was like at home. She remembered one thing above all: he never entered the house without praying two units, and never left without praying two. He never broke that habit.

There is a quiet lesson in him. Take the gift you have been given and put it to good use. Take a warning personally instead of passing it on. And let small, steady habits hold your heart in place.

May Allah be pleased with him, and gather us in the company of those whose worship never slipped.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Abdullah ibn Rawahah (may Allah be pleased with him) lived in the city that would come to be called Madinah, and he was known for two things. He could read and write, which was rare among his people, and his mind was very quick. He could glance at coins and name their worth, and look at a stranger and tell you where he was from.

He was not a man who was easily fooled. So when he first saw the Prophet ﷺ, the whole city wondered what he would say. He looked, and he simply knew. Even without the signs, he said, you would only have to watch the way this man carries himself to know he is a prophet. He accepted Islam at once.

He became a poet for the Prophet ﷺ, and a warrior too, which was a strange pairing in those days. He chanted his verses not from the back but from the very front of the battle. When the Muslims dug a great trench around the city, frightened and hungry, the Prophet ﷺ lifted his voice with Abdullah's words, and soon every man was chanting them as he dug.

His worship was as strong as his courage. He liked to take a friend by the arm and say, "Come, let us believe for an hour." He meant: let us sit and remember Allah together. Once he read a verse warning of poets led astray and wept, afraid it was about him, until he reached its ending and found mercy there.

He died at the Battle of Mutah. The Prophet ﷺ had named three leaders: Zayd, then Ja'far, then Abdullah. When the first two were killed and the banner came to him, he felt himself hesitate. So he spoke to his own soul. "Do I see you resisting Paradise? By Allah, you will enter it." Then he carried the banner forward and was killed.
When the news reached Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ wept, and climbed the pulpit in tears to name the three who had been taken.

After he was gone, his widow was asked what he was like at home. She remembered one thing above all: he never came into the house without praying two units of prayer, and never left without praying two. He never broke that habit, not once.
storybook.pullquote: Do I see you resisting Paradise? By Allah, you will enter it.
storybook.reflection: Abdullah had every gift that could make a man proud, and he spent them all in service while still fearing he had not done enough. Take what you have been given and put it to good use, take a warning to heart instead of passing it on, and keep the quiet habits that hold a soul steady. That, more than any verse he wrote, is what he leaves us.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
